title: AI in Project Management
summary: The 2026 exam treats artificial intelligence as part of everyday project work. AI is a driver behind the PMBOK 8 update, showing up inside ordinary scenarios across the Governance, Risk and Monitoring and Control domains. The exam tests your judgement in using it - and whether you stay accountable for the output - not how the tools work.
intro: Use AI on projects while staying accountable for the output.

sections.heading: AI as a 2026 exam driver
sections.body: PMI named artificial intelligence as one of the trends driving the 2026 update, so AI shows up inside ordinary scenarios across the performance domains - not as a separate topic. You will not be asked how an algorithm works. You will be asked what a project manager should do when AI is involved.

blocks.title: The through-line
lines: AI is a powerful assistant, but the project manager stays accountable for the decision.
Treat AI like a very fast junior analyst: useful for a first draft, never the final authority.
This maps to the Be an Accountable Leader principle - stay accountable, keep a human in the loop.

rows.value: AI surfaces status, patterns and anomalies in data - you still judge and decide.
AI is both a tool for risk analysis and a source of new risks to manage.
AI automates routine execution and assists drafting; value delivery stays the point.
Stay accountable for AI output; human-in-the-loop and data ethics are non-negotiable.

sections.heading: Automation, Assistant, Augmentation
sections.body: PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition addresses AI in an appendix and frames three modes of use. A rule of thumb: automate execution, augment decisions, but never delegate accountability. The further a use moves from routine execution toward judgement, the more human oversight it needs.

groups.label: What it does
lines: AI performs repetitive, rules-based execution on its own.

groups.label: Examples
lines: Generating routine reports.
Updating logs.

groups.label: Payoff
lines: Frees the team for higher-value work.

groups.label: What it does
lines: AI acts like a junior team member that drafts or proposes.
A person reviews and approves before anything is used.

groups.label: Examples
lines: A first-draft estimate.
A status update.
A candidate risk list.

groups.label: What it does
lines: AI enhances a person's own judgement.
It surfaces options, patterns and analysis.
The manager then weighs and decides.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Match the mode to the task: automate the routine, let AI assist on drafts, augment your judgement on decisions.
Whatever the mode, you still own the outcome.

sections.heading: Where AI genuinely helps
sections.body: AI can accelerate real project work. In each case AI produces an input the project manager evaluates - it speeds up the work, it does not own the outcome.

items.title: Estimating and scheduling
lines: Drafting estimates.
Spotting schedule risk.
Suggesting sequences.

items.title: Reporting and communication
lines: Summarising status.
Drafting stakeholder updates.

items.title: Risk and data analysis
lines: Surfacing patterns, candidate risks and anomalies in large data sets.

blocks.title: Answer style
lines: Favour answers where AI augments the team's analysis, not ones where it replaces the project manager's decision.
Value delivery is still the measure of success - AI is a means, not the end.

sections.heading: You stay accountable - human oversight
sections.body: Accountability cannot be delegated to a tool. AI output is a recommendation to verify against project context, data and expert judgement before acting. If an AI suggestion conflicts with the team's knowledge or the project's reality, investigate the discrepancy rather than defer to the tool.

blocks.title: The trap
lines: Blindly implementing an AI-generated estimate, schedule or risk response is the wrong answer.
Letting AI make a stakeholder decision is the wrong answer.
'Act on the AI output immediately' is almost always a trap.

blocks.title: The mindset
lines: 'Validate it, then decide' is the human-in-the-loop mindset PMBOK 8 rewards.
When AI conflicts with reality, investigate the discrepancy - don't defer to the tool.

sections.heading: Data quality and bias
sections.body: AI is only as good as the data behind it. Incomplete, outdated or biased data produces confident-but-wrong output. Before relying on an AI result, the project manager should consider where the data came from, whether it is representative, and what it might be missing. This matters most for decisions affecting people and stakeholders, where biased data can lead to unfair or non-compliant outcomes.

blocks.title: Garbage in, garbage out
lines: When an AI result looks surprising, question the data and assumptions first.
Ask: where did the data come from, is it representative, what might it be missing?
Biased data affecting people is a data-ethics and accountable-leadership concern.

sections.heading: Ethics, privacy and security
sections.body: Using AI raises governance questions the project manager must respect. When organizational AI policy exists, follow it; when it doesn't, escalate to establish one rather than improvising.

items.title: Transparency
lines: Disclose where AI was used in analysis or deliverables.

items.title: Data privacy and security
lines: Don't feed confidential or personal data into tools that aren't approved for it.

items.title: Intellectual property and compliance
lines: Follow organizational policy, contracts and regulations.

items.title: Ethics
lines: Align with the PMI Code of Ethics: responsibility, respect, fairness, honesty.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: If a scenario hints at confidential data going into a public AI tool, the safe answer protects privacy and checks policy first.
No policy yet? Escalate to establish one rather than improvising.

sections.heading: AI as a risk and a stakeholder concern
sections.body: AI introduces its own risks that belong in the risk register and response planning. It also creates stakeholder concerns, including fears about job impact and trust in automated decisions. Manage both the way you would any risk and any stakeholder issue: identify, analyse, plan responses, and engage people openly to build understanding.

groups.label: Threats to log
lines: Inaccurate output.
Security exposure.
Over-reliance.
Unclear accountability.

groups.label: Handle it
lines: Identify, analyse, plan responses like any risk.

groups.label: Concerns
lines: Fears about job impact.
Trust in automated decisions.

groups.label: Handle it
lines: Engage people openly to build understanding.

blocks.title: People tip
lines: Address AI anxiety with engagement and transparency.
Don't ignore it, and don't force adoption.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
